title: Controlling Transient Amplification Improves Long-horizon Rollouts
text: arXiv 2026

title: Controlling Transient Amplification Improves Long-horizon Rollouts
section: The gap between one step and many
text:
The gap between one step and many
Neural networks are increasingly used to simulate physical systems — the weather, ocean currents, turbulent fluids, molecules. The common recipe is to train a network to take one small step forward in time: given the state of the system now, predict what it looks like a moment later. To forecast far into the future, you simply run the network in a loop, feeding each prediction back in as the next input. Trained this way, these models have become remarkably accurate and often run orders of magnitude faster than the classical solvers they imitate (Pathak et al. 2022; Lam et al. 2023; Kochkov et al. 2024; Sanchez-Gonzalez et al. 2020).

Lam, Remi, Alvaro Sanchez-Gonzalez, Matthew Willson, et al. 2023. “Learning Skillful Medium-Range Global Weather Forecasting.” Science 382 (6677). https://doi.org/10.1126/science.adi2336.

Kochkov, Dmitrii, Janni Yuval, Ian Langmore, et al. 2024. “Neural General Circulation Models for Weather and Climate.” Nature 632: 1060–66.

Sanchez-Gonzalez, Alvaro, Jonathan Godwin, Tobias Pfaff, Rex Ying, Jure Leskovec, and Peter W. Battaglia. 2020. “Learning to Simulate Complex Physics with Graph Networks.” International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML).
The trouble is the loop itself. Each prediction carries a small error, and as the model consumes its own output step after step, accuracy degrades far faster than the single-step error would suggest: a model that nearly matches the ground truth after one step can drift or blow up within tens of steps (Lippe et al. 2023). Closing this gap between one step and many is the central open problem for neural simulators of dynamics.
The usual explanation is distributional shift — the model trains on clean data but rolls out on its own imperfect outputs, which slowly wander off the training distribution. Methods that expose the model to its own errors during training (Lippe et al. 2023) or correct predictions at inference (Kohl et al. 2026) help, but they treat drift as a data or inference problem. They do not explain the structural mechanism that makes a small error grow in the first place. This work is complementary to those methods, not a replacement: it targets the structural component of error growth, and the two should combine.

Lippe, Phillip, Bastiaan S. Veeling, Paris Perdikaris, Richard E. Turner, and Johannes Brandstetter. 2023. “PDE-Refiner: Achieving Accurate Long Rollouts with Neural PDE Solvers.” Thirty-Seventh Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS). https://openreview.net/forum?id=Qv6468llWS.

Kohl, Georg, Li-Wei Chen, and Nils Thuerey. 2026. “Benchmarking Autoregressive Conditional Diffusion Models for Turbulent Flow Simulation.” Neural Networks 199: 108641. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neunet.2026.108641.

title: Controlling Transient Amplification Improves Long-horizon Rollouts
text:
Transient amplification
The mechanism we point to is a classical phenomenon from dynamical systems called transient amplification. The idea is best seen on a simple stable system perturbed away from equilibrium. Asymptotic stability only guarantees that the perturbation eventually dies out — it says nothing about what happens on the way there.







Figure 1: Stable. Orthogonal directions of decay (a normal system). The perturbation shrinks monotonically.









Figure 2: Unstable. One direction genuinely grows. The perturbation diverges without bound.









Figure 3: Transient. The same stable eigenvalues as the first case, but nearly parallel directions (a non-normal system). The norm first grows to a peak well above its starting value before decaying.




All three systems above are built from the same kind of linear update; what differs is the geometry of their directions and whether they grow or shrink. The first two cases are the familiar ones. When the directions are orthogonal and contracting (a stable, normal system), a perturbation simply decays. When one direction genuinely grows (an unstable system), the perturbation diverges without bound — the obvious failure mode, and the one stability analysis is built to rule out.
The third case is the subtle one, and the point of this work. It has the same stable eigenvalues as the first, so it is still asymptotically stable — it always returns to equilibrium. But its directions are nearly parallel (a non-normal system), and on the way back, the nearly-cancelling projections briefly reinforce one another and the perturbation is amplified, sometimes by a large factor, before it decays (Trefethen and Embree 2005). A system can be perfectly stable and still blow a small error up by a large factor first. This is exactly the regime that matters in practice, because rollouts are judged at tens to hundreds of steps — the medium horizon where transients dominate. Enforcing stability alone does not protect against it.

Trefethen, Lloyd N., and Mark Embree. 2005. Spectra and Pseudospectra: The Behavior of Nonnormal Matrices and Operators. Princeton University Press.

title: Controlling Transient Amplification Improves Long-horizon Rollouts
section: From dynamical systems to neural networks
text:
From dynamical systems to neural networks
The same picture transfers to a neural simulator through linearization. Following its prediction error from one step to the next, the error is advanced by the model’s local Jacobian — the linear map describing how the network responds to a small change in its input. Over a rollout the error is therefore passed through a product of these Jacobians, one per step, and it is the behavior of this product that decides whether errors stay small or amplify.
Two properties of those Jacobians keep transients in check. First, each Jacobian should be close to normal, so that no single step amplifies errors (the within-step story above). Second, the Jacobians at successive steps should commute, so their directions of stretching stay aligned over time; otherwise a direction that one step contracts can be re-expanded by the next. Standard training rewards neither property, and unregularized networks trained on non-normal dynamics tend to learn Jacobians that are both non-normal and non-commuting.







Figure 4: Commutativity regularization acts on the latent advance map \mathbf{z}_t \mapsto \mathbf{z}_{t+1} (the U-Net bottleneck, or the middle FNO layers), leaving the inference path untouched.




Commutativity regularization adds two penalties during training to encourage exactly these properties: a normality penalty on each latent Jacobian and a commutator penalty on consecutive Jacobians. The two are complementary — controlling one without the other leaves either within-step or cross-step amplification uncontrolled. Both are estimated cheaply with Jacobian-vector products, never forming any matrix explicitly, and the rollout at inference is unchanged, so there is no inference-time cost. A propagator bound in the paper makes this precise: as the penalties shrink, long-horizon error decays at the rate set by the Jacobians’ spectral radius rather than their (generally larger) spectral norm. We refer the reader to the paper for the full method and theory.

title: Controlling Transient Amplification Improves Long-horizon Rollouts
text:
Long-horizon rollouts in practice
We evaluate the regularizer across settings spanning 1D and 2D, integrable and chaotic, synthetic and real. In each case an otherwise-identical baseline is compared against a model with the two penalties added, and rollouts are run far beyond the training window. The recurring trade: a small amount of one-step accuracy is exchanged for a bounded, far more accurate long-horizon rollout.
KdV (1D). Trained on a short window of single-soliton dynamics and rolled out to many times that horizon, the regularized U-Net stays accurate where the baseline drifts. The effect is sharpest out-of-distribution: on initial conditions with several interacting solitons — never seen in training — the baseline degrades badly while the regularized model is largely unaffected.





In-distribution — initial condition (single soliton)







In-distribution — U-Net (baseline)






In-distribution — U-Net + Comm. Reg. (ours)








Out-of-distribution — initial condition (multiple solitons)







Out-of-distribution — U-Net (baseline)






Out-of-distribution — U-Net + Comm. Reg. (ours)





Barotropic vorticity (2D, chaotic). On this dissipative, chaotic fluid the unregularized U-Net destabilizes inside the training horizon, saturating into a field decorrelated from the truth. The same regularizer that controls slow drift on KdV here converts an instability into a bounded, on-attractor rollout.





Barotropic vorticity rollout: ground truth, baseline, and regularized U-Net.



FourCastNet on ERA5 (real, large-scale). Starting from the pretrained FourCastNet weather model (Pathak et al. 2022), we fine-tune on a few in-distribution years of its own training data. Plain fine-tuning worsens the long-horizon near-surface temperature forecast; the same data with commutativity regularization turns that regression into a substantial improvement over the frozen model — no new data, no inference-time cost.

Pathak, Jaideep, Shashank Subramanian, Peter Harrington, et al. 2022. “FourCastNet: A Global Data-Driven High-Resolution Weather Model Using Adaptive Fourier Neural Operators.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:2202.11214. https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.11214.





Near-surface temperature (t_\mathrm{2m}) rollout error on held-out ERA5: frozen vs. plain fine-tune vs. regularized fine-tune.

title: Controlling Transient Amplification Improves Long-horizon Rollouts
text:
Takeaways
Long-horizon rollout error is not only a data problem. A stable neural simulator can still amplify its own errors transiently, and non-normal, non-commuting latent Jacobians are a structural driver of that amplification — one that enforcing stability alone does not address. Commutativity regularization controls the mechanism directly with two Jacobian-vector-product penalties at zero inference cost, and it is complementary to refinement and diffusion methods that target distributional shift. For the propagator bound, the full method, and the complete experiments, we invite you to read the paper.

title: CLAI Research Group
text:
Locatello Group · ISTA



Today’s models describe the world as they found it. We want AI that grasps why — learning causal representations and models from data, so it can reason about interventions, domain shifts, and the structure beneath what it sees.

Browse papers Meet the team →
We learn causal structure from data, so that AI can reason about interventions and distribution shifts rather than only fit the conditions it was trained on. Our work spans:

title: About
text:
Modern machine learning is still limited to a superficial description of reality that only holds when the experimental conditions are fixed. It largely ignores interventions in the world, domain shifts, and temporal structure. Our group focuses on learning causal representations and causal models from data to address these issues. Through rigorous theory and scalable algorithms, we enable AI agents to understand cause-and-effect relationships, the effect of interventions, and distribution changes underlying the data they encounter.
Advances in causal learning have promising applications in machine learning and artificial intelligence, including robustness, explainability, and fairness in recognition, reasoning, and planning tasks. Most importantly, their impact extends to discovering new scientific knowledge from massive amounts of data and serving as the interface between people and complex systems for decision-making.
Causal learning emerges by discovering structural knowledge about the data-generating process and meaningful abstractions that allow for well-defined causal relations. Research questions span theoretical identifiability and methodological innovations in causal learning, discovering objects and abstract entities, their factors of variations, and their causal relations. We apply these methods to solve open problems in machine learning and enable new applications in the sciences.

Contact

Institute group page: ista.ac.at/en/research/locatello-group

Controlling Transient Amplification Improves Long-horizon Rollouts



AI for Science

Dynamical Systems

Theory



Autoregressive neural simulators match classical solvers on short-horizon prediction but degrade rapidly over long rollouts. This work identifies transient amplification of perturbations along rollout trajectories as a structural driver of that error: when the Jacobians along an autoregressive trajectory are non-normal and non-commuting, errors are amplified transiently even when the system is asymptotically stable. The authors propose commutativity regularization — a commutator penalty and a normality penalty, both estimated with Jacobian-vector products and carrying no inference-time cost — and prove a propagator bound that replaces the per-step spectral-norm decay rate with the shared spectral radius under approximate commutativity and normality. On UNet and FNO variants across the 1D KdV equation, the 2D barotropic vorticity equation, sea-surface temperature, and fine-tuning of FourCastNet on ERA5, the method sustains long-horizon rollouts over thousands of steps, with the largest gains out-of-distribution.





May 9, 2026


Adeel Pervez, Francesco Locatello


arXiv


2026
